title: Apocalypse Cow

jokes.text: Hey, hey, kids! It's a beautiful, sunny Saturday morning. So get ready for six hours of cartoons!
jokes.setup: Enthusiastic Saturday morning cartoon host greeting
jokes.punchline: The absurd commitment of six straight hours of cartoons
jokes.explanation: Classic Krusty character comedy - the enthusiastic host pushing absurd amounts of content to kids. Well-structured setup/punchline with good economy.

jokes.text: Trans-Clown-O-Morphs Transforming clowns that morph Sent to Earth to defeat the evil Dino-Robo-Bots
jokes.setup: Serious action-cartoon narrator voice describing the premise
jokes.punchline: The inherently ridiculous concept of transforming clowns vs robot dinosaurs
jokes.explanation: Solid parody of over-the-top action cartoon premises. The absurd juxtaposition of clowns and robots works well.

jokes.text: Buy my cereal, find the secret code inside and text me the message 'start the day with Kellogg's' - or I will die.
jokes.setup: Heroic cartoon character asking for help from viewers
jokes.punchline: The blatant product placement and death threat to children
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of manipulative children's advertising. The death threat escalation is perfectly uncomfortable and highlights how exploitative these tactics are.

jokes.text: Hurry! Guess no one's buying my cereal. Buy or I'll die.
jokes.setup: Character already threatening death if kids don't buy cereal
jokes.punchline: The increasingly desperate and manipulative pleading
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the cereal death threat. The economy is excellent - 'Buy or I'll die' is perfectly blunt.

jokes.text: Every week we have to buy something else to save that transforming clown that morphs.
jokes.setup: Marge commenting on the manipulative cartoon tactics
jokes.punchline: The redundant description 'transforming clown that morphs' showing her confusion
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge character moment - she's trying to understand the nonsensical cartoon but gets confused by the redundant naming. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Mom, we need a Callaway titanium perimeter-weighted driver! - It adds yards to any swing.
jokes.setup: Marge just complained about commercial manipulation, then golf is on
jokes.punchline: Bart immediately falls for golf equipment advertising using exact commercial language
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback structure - immediately after Marge's complaint about ads, Bart proves her point. The technical golf jargon is perfectly ridiculous.

jokes.text: Lisa, I've got a big bunch of black banas. You know what that means. Bana bread!
jokes.setup: Marge announcing she has overripe bananas
jokes.punchline: Her mispronunciation 'banas' repeated in 'bana bread'
jokes.explanation: Classic Marge verbal quirk. The mispronunciation is endearing rather than brilliant, but fits her character perfectly.

jokes.text: Oh, no, I hate the beanbag store. The clerks are always shouting in Hebrew on their cell phones.
jokes.setup: Bart complaining about going to get beanbag chairs re-beaned
jokes.punchline: The oddly specific complaint about Hebrew-speaking clerks
jokes.explanation: Weirdly specific observation that feels very Simpsons-esque. The randomness of the complaint is what makes it funny.

jokes.text: Don't you worry about Wikipedia. We'll change it when we get home. We'll change a lot of things.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Dean Martin's work habits contradicting Wikipedia
jokes.punchline: Homer's plan to just edit Wikipedia to match their conversation, plus the ominous 'a lot of things'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant modern satire about Wikipedia editing. Homer's casual approach to changing 'facts' and the escalation to 'a lot of things' is perfectly in character and highly quotable.

jokes.text: Hey, check it out! I wouldn't mind driving that over a prairie dog village.
jokes.setup: Homer seeing a large farm vehicle
jokes.punchline: His immediate thought is using it to massacre prairie dogs
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark Homer moment. The casual violence toward cute animals is shocking but perfectly in character for his impulsive, destructive thoughts.

jokes.text: Martin?! What's a tool like you doing on something cool like that?
jokes.setup: Bart seeing Martin operating impressive farm equipment
jokes.punchline: The contradiction between Martin being a 'tool' but doing something 'cool'
jokes.explanation: Standard Bart bullying of Martin, but the setup works because it acknowledges the equipment actually is cool. Decent character comedy.

jokes.text: Indeed, it has the most finger loss of any youth organization.
jokes.setup: Bart asking if 4-H is dangerous
jokes.punchline: Martin's matter-of-fact delivery of this horrifying statistic
jokes.explanation: Exceptional dark comedy delivered with perfect Martin deadpan. The phrase 'most finger loss of any youth organization' is brilliantly specific and disturbing while being delivered as casual trivia.

jokes.text: Son, a lot of these youth organizations encourage parents to get involved in the activities. - Not this one. - Sold!
jokes.setup: Homer explaining why he might not want Bart in 4-H due to parental involvement
jokes.punchline: His instant enthusiasm when told parents don't get involved
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer parenting moment. The structure builds tension then releases it perfectly with 'Sold!' His lazy parenting style is perfectly captured.

jokes.text: I pledge my head to clearer thinking, my heart to greater loyalty, my hands.. oh, man, how many 'H's' does this thing have?
jokes.setup: Bart reciting the formal 4-H pledge
jokes.punchline: Getting overwhelmed by the alliteration halfway through
jokes.explanation: Good Bart moment showing his impatience with formal proceedings. The breaking of the fourth wall about the H's works well.

jokes.text: Raise articulator, set hydraulic brakes and endorse farm subsidy check for crops I didn't grow.
jokes.setup: Homer operating complex farm machinery
jokes.punchline: The progression from mechanical operations to casual government fraud
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from technical farm operations to casual admission of subsidy fraud. The matter-of-fact delivery makes the illegal activity even funnier.

jokes.text: I didn't come here today expecting to fall in love.
jokes.setup: Bart at the calf selection, presumably looking for a tough animal
jokes.punchline: His romantic language about choosing a farm animal
jokes.explanation: Sweet character moment that sets up Bart's attachment to Lou. The romantic language applied to livestock is endearing rather than creepy.

jokes.text: looks like we're partners. You're a scrappy little misfit, just like me.
jokes.setup: Bart stuck with the runt calf nobody wanted
jokes.punchline: His identification with the runt as a fellow misfit
jokes.explanation: Good character development moment. Bart's self-awareness about being a misfit is touching and sets up his bond with Lou.

jokes.text: Anyone want to trade? No reasonable offer refused. I'll take a chicken.
jokes.setup: Bart trying to get rid of the runt calf
jokes.punchline: Escalating desperation from 'no reasonable offer refused' to accepting a chicken
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure showing Bart's immediate buyer's remorse. The progression to accepting any poultry is nicely desperate.

jokes.text: Oh, I thought she was peeing out of her nipple.
jokes.setup: Mary calling the male calf 'Lulubelle' and being corrected
jokes.punchline: Her innocent but anatomically confused explanation
jokes.explanation: Perfectly innocent but uncomfortable line that establishes Mary's farm naivety. The anatomical confusion is both gross and endearing.

jokes.text: That's great. It rhymes 'moo.'
jokes.setup: Bart suggesting they call the calf 'Lou' instead of 'Lulubelle'
jokes.punchline: Mary's delighted observation about the rhyme with the cow sound
jokes.explanation: Sweet character moment showing Mary's simple pleasure in wordplay. Not brilliant but fits her innocent character perfectly.

jokes.text: Now son, I'm not here to judge. - Actually, yes, you are. - Eh, what? Oh, right, I am.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to be supportive about Bart's calf
jokes.punchline: Being reminded he's literally there as a judge, his confusion, then acceptance
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment of confusion followed by acceptance. The literal vs. figurative 'judging' creates good misdirection.

jokes.text: But he won! Why don't you kill the loser cows?
jokes.setup: Bart learning his prize-winning calf will be slaughtered
jokes.punchline: His childlike logic that winners should be spared while losers die
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy from a child's perspective. Bart's logic makes perfect sense from his viewpoint, making the reality even more disturbing.

jokes.text: Oh, we will, but your cow gets to go first.
jokes.setup: Bart asking why they don't kill the losing cows
jokes.punchline: The casual revelation that ALL cows will be killed, winners just get priority
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark escalation. The matter-of-fact delivery makes the horrible reality even more disturbing and funny.

jokes.text: Mom, they're going to kill Lou and make him into food and fringe vests for gay cowboys.
jokes.setup: Bart pleading with Marge to save Lou
jokes.punchline: The progression from food to the oddly specific 'fringe vests for gay cowboys'
jokes.explanation: The escalation from practical (food) to absurd (fringe vests for gay cowboys) is perfectly random Simpsons humor. The specificity makes it memorably weird.

jokes.text: Son, let this be a lesson: never work hard and don't form emotional attachments. Also, don't be a cow.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to comfort Bart with life advice
jokes.punchline: The progression from cynical life lessons to the absurd 'don't be a cow'
jokes.explanation: Peak Homer wisdom. The escalation from terrible life advice to the absurd final point is perfect. This encapsulates Homer's parenting style beautifully and is endlessly quotable.

jokes.text: Leave me alone! It's not my fault there's nothing good to eat on this planet except meat!
jokes.setup: Bart imagining he hears Lou's voice
jokes.punchline: His defensive justification expanding from personal guilt to cosmic blame
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from personal guilt to blaming the entire planet's food chain. Shows Bart's typical deflection tactics.

jokes.text: If I could save Lou, I'd never eat meat again. Not even prosciutto and melon!
jokes.setup: Bart making a desperate promise to become vegetarian
jokes.punchline: The escalation to giving up even the fancy Italian delicacy
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'prosciutto and melon' is funny - suggesting Bart has sophisticated taste while also showing the depth of his sacrifice.

jokes.text: That wasn't my conscience mooing, it was... Tress MacNeille!
jokes.setup: Lisa suggesting Bart heard his conscience
jokes.punchline: Bart realizing it was actually the voice actor from the video Lisa planted
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-joke naming the actual voice actress. The revelation that it wasn't his conscience but a planted video is a good twist.

jokes.text: Sorry, Lis. I can't be a vegetarian. I love the taste of death.
jokes.setup: Lisa trying to convince Bart to become vegetarian
jokes.punchline: Bart's poetic but morbid description of his carnivorous nature
jokes.explanation: Brilliant line that's both disturbing and hilarious. 'I love the taste of death' is perfectly Bart - honest about his nature but expressed in an unexpectedly poetic way.

jokes.text: Stop stirring your lemonade with a sausage.
jokes.setup: Lisa trying to get Bart to compromise on meat consumption
jokes.punchline: The absurd image of Bart using sausage as a stirring stick
jokes.explanation: Perfectly absurd visual gag that shows Bart's meat obsession taken to ridiculous extremes. The image is both disgusting and hilarious.

jokes.text: Milk is murder. Cheese is genocide.
jokes.setup: Lisa giving signal to animal activists
jokes.punchline: Escalating rhetoric from murder to genocide
jokes.explanation: Good escalation in activist rhetoric, economical delivery of escalating absurdity

jokes.text: Aw, man, I never figured the gate would be locked. It's like they knew we were coming.
jokes.setup: Bart and activists arriving at the feedlot to rescue Lou
jokes.punchline: Bart's surprise that a commercial facility would have basic security
jokes.explanation: Classic Bart naivety about how the world works. His surprise at basic security measures is endearingly childish.

jokes.text: Yeah, it's my code name. This is Compost and this is Solar Panel. What's my code name? Lisa's Brother.
jokes.setup: Environmental activists introducing their nature-based code names
jokes.punchline: Bart getting the mundane 'Lisa's Brother' as his code name
jokes.explanation: Good setup/punchline structure. The contrast between the activists' pretentious nature names and Bart's literal description is nicely deflating.

jokes.text: Lisa's Brother.
jokes.setup: Bart asking what his code name is
jokes.punchline: Completely mundane descriptive name instead of cool code name
jokes.explanation: Good subversion of cool code name expectations, economical punchline

jokes.text: Forgive us for this violation, Mother Fence. We honor your chain-link spirit.
jokes.setup: Environmental activist cutting through fence to rescue animals
jokes.punchline: Apologizing to and spiritualizing the fence itself
jokes.explanation: Perfect parody of over-the-top environmental spirituality. The idea of honoring a chain-link fence's 'spirit' while cutting it is delightfully absurd.

jokes.text: I don't care how much of a pumped-up freak you are. I still love you. The way Barry Bonds' kids probably still love him.
jokes.setup: Bart reassuring Lou despite his hormone-enhanced size
jokes.punchline: The analogy to Barry Bonds and steroid controversy
jokes.explanation: Clever contemporary reference connecting cow growth hormones to baseball steroid scandals. The 'probably' adds perfect uncertainty about family loyalty.

jokes.text: That was a practice cow.
jokes.setup: Bart successfully operating complex farm equipment to move Lou
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation that he screwed up with a different cow first
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that recontextualizes his apparent competence. The casual admission fits Bart's character perfectly.

jokes.text: Howdy, what's yours that mine's is Cletus?
jokes.setup: Cletus greeting Bart at the door
jokes.punchline: His backwards, grammatically confused way of asking for Bart's name
jokes.explanation: Classic Cletus verbal confusion that perfectly establishes his character. The backwards grammar is endearingly wrong.

jokes.text: She had a regular city birth in a gas station.
jokes.setup: Bart saying he didn't know Cletus was Mary's dad
jokes.punchline: Cletus's proud description of what he considers a 'regular city birth'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Cletus line that shows his skewed perspective on what constitutes civilization. A gas station birth as 'regular city' is beautifully wrong.

jokes.text: According to the traditions of the hill folk, by giving our daughter a cow, you've made a formal proposal of marriage.
jokes.setup: Bart just trying to give away his cow
jokes.punchline: The bizarre cultural tradition that makes this a marriage proposal
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist premise that drives the episode's conflict. The idea that cow-giving equals marriage proposal is perfectly ridiculous.

jokes.text: I can't get married. I'm only ten. And I have dreams. I got 1580 on my DQAT.
jokes.setup: Bart protesting the marriage with reasonable objections
jokes.punchline: Revealing his dreams are about the Dairy Queen Aptitude Test
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection from age concerns to fake academic achievement. The DQAT being Bart's dream is perfectly lowbrow.

jokes.text: DQAT? - Dairy Queen Aptitude Test. 800 ice cream, 780 Brazier.
jokes.setup: Mary asking what DQAT means
jokes.punchline: The revelation it's a fake SAT for fast food, complete with section scores
jokes.explanation: Brilliant parody of standardized testing applied to fast food. The detailed section scores make it even more absurd and believable.

jokes.text: I wished I had a tail what did swap away flies. Mine just hangs there.
jokes.setup: Mary watching Lou happily swat flies with his tail
jokes.punchline: Her innocent envy of functional animal anatomy
jokes.explanation: Perfectly innocent but disturbing line about human anatomy. Mary's matter-of-fact delivery makes it even more unsettling.

jokes.text: We always figured someday Mary would marry. That's why we called her Mary.
jokes.setup: Cletus explaining their marriage expectations
jokes.punchline: The implication that 'Mary' is specifically a marrying name
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Cletus's simple logic about names determining destiny. The wordplay on 'Mary/marry' is subtle but effective.

jokes.text: We name all our kids after what we think's gonna happen to 'em. Ain't that right, Stabbed-in-jail?
jokes.setup: Cletus explaining their naming philosophy
jokes.punchline: The horrifically specific name 'Stabbed-in-jail'
jokes.explanation: Exceptionally dark comedy that escalates from the innocent 'Mary' example to something horrifically specific. The matter-of-fact delivery makes it even funnier.

jokes.text: We'll see who stabs who.
jokes.setup: Being introduced as 'Stabbed-in-jail'
jokes.punchline: The threatening response suggesting he won't be passive in his predicted fate
jokes.explanation: Perfect follow-up to the previous joke. The defiant response to his predetermined fate is both threatening and hilarious.

jokes.text: What was that for? - Bart's in trouble and we don't need your stupid comments. - Zippin' it up.
jokes.setup: Homer apparently hitting Ned for no reason
jokes.punchline: Homer's preemptive strike against Ned's expected commentary, Ned's immediate compliance
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer-Ned dynamic. Homer's preemptive violence and Ned's immediate submission is classic character comedy.

jokes.text: Something old... something new, something burrowed, and something stew.
jokes.setup: Traditional wedding saying about 'something borrowed'
jokes.punchline: Hillbilly corruption to 'burrowed' and adding 'something stew'
jokes.explanation: Clever corruption of the traditional saying that fits the hillbilly characters. 'Something stew' as a wedding tradition is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: you just got cold feet... 'cause you're barefoot and our floor's made of mud.
jokes.setup: Mary having pre-wedding anxiety
jokes.punchline: Brandine's literal interpretation of 'cold feet' due to their living conditions
jokes.explanation: Good literal interpretation of an idiom that reveals their living conditions. The logic is both wrong and perfectly reasonable.

jokes.text: Kiss?! I thought we were just getting married.
jokes.setup: Wedding ceremony approaching the kiss moment
jokes.punchline: Bart's innocent confusion about what marriage involves
jokes.explanation: Perfect childish innocence about adult relationships. The idea that marriage doesn't involve kissing is endearingly naive.

jokes.text: And since we don't need to hear from women folk what they think, I now pronounce you...
jokes.setup: Traditional wedding ceremony format
jokes.punchline: Casual dismissal of the bride's consent as unnecessary
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy about archaic gender attitudes. The casual dismissal of women's opinions is both horrible and funny in its bluntness.

jokes.text: He still sits in the driveway and pretends to drive the car.
jokes.setup: Marge arguing Bart is too young to marry
jokes.punchline: The specific, embarrassing detail about his childish behavior
jokes.explanation: Perfect specific detail that shows Bart's immaturity. The image is both endearing and embarrassing.

jokes.text: Don't feel bad, sweetie. I was 13 when I married your father. - Yeah, and you'd already been divorced four times.
jokes.setup: Brandine trying to comfort Mary about being an old maid at 11
jokes.punchline: The revelation of her extremely early marriage and multiple divorces by 13
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation that makes the 'comfort' even worse. The math of multiple divorces by age 13 is disturbingly funny.

jokes.text: Sweetie, don't you think your mom might have a trick up her sleeve? - No. - Trust me. - D'oh! I mean, moo!
jokes.setup: Homer trying to hint that Marge has a plan
jokes.punchline: His accidental 'D'oh!' followed by desperate cow sound cover-up
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment of trying to be clever but revealing himself. The desperate 'moo!' cover-up is endearingly stupid.

jokes.text: Ooh, 'Laughter House.' With the world in the shape it's in, we could all use some comedy.
jokes.setup: Homer reading what he thinks is a comedy venue sign
jokes.punchline: His optimistic interpretation of what's actually 'Slaughter House'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy through Homer's innocent misreading. The irony of finding optimism in a slaughterhouse is perfectly Homer.

jokes.text: In India, your cow will be treated like a god and receive the food and medical care that in America would go to human beings.
jokes.setup: Marge explaining where Lou is going
jokes.punchline: The cutting comparison of American healthcare vs Indian cow worship
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire about American healthcare delivered through cow worship comparison. The observation is both funny and uncomfortably true.

jokes.text: Lou, if that plane leaves the ground and you're not on it, you'll regret it. Maybe not today, maybe not tomorrow, but soon, and definitely after they kill you and make you into sloppy joes.
jokes.setup: Classic Casablanca reference format
jokes.punchline: The escalation from romantic movie quote to meat processing reality
jokes.explanation: Excellent movie reference parody that maintains the dramatic structure while adding dark cow-specific reality. The 'sloppy joes' ending is perfectly crude.

jokes.text: Here's looking at you, cud.
jokes.setup: Another Casablanca reference to Lou
jokes.punchline: The pun replacing 'kid' with cow-specific 'cud'
jokes.explanation: Perfect pun that works both as wordplay and character moment. The economy is excellent - just three words that completely land the joke.

jokes.text: that sounds like Tress MacNeille.
jokes.setup: Homer hearing cow voices at the slaughterhouse
jokes.punchline: Recognition of the voice actress from the earlier planted video
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier meta-joke about the voice actress. Not as strong as the original but provides nice continuity.

jokes.text: It makes me never want to eat meat again. Just fish, chicken, burgers, veal on Fridays, deer, but only in season and if necessary, the sweetest meat of all: human.
jokes.setup: Homer claiming the slaughterhouse experience changed him about meat
jokes.punchline: The increasingly contradictory list ending with cannibalism
jokes.explanation: Peak Homer comedy with perfect escalation structure. The progression from 'never eat meat' through increasingly specific exceptions to cannibalism is brilliantly written and perfectly in character.

jokes.text: You're in a better place now, Lou, and I'll always be proud that for once in my life, I had a cow, man.
jokes.setup: Bart's touching farewell to Lou
jokes.punchline: The pun on Bart's catchphrase 'Don't have a cow, man' reversed to 'I had a cow, man'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback to Bart's most famous catchphrase used in a touching, literal context. The reversal from 'don't have a cow' to 'I had a cow' is perfect closure for the episode.

tmdb_overview: Bart joins the 4-H club and raises a calf to be a blue-ribbon-winning bull, but panics when he learns the bull will be slaughtered.
